what: The 'Local Development' section is the only README content aimed at contributors working from a checkout, but it never states how to obtain the checkout in the first place. There is no `git clone <url>` command, and no repository URL appears anywhere in the document (the only hint is an incidental GitHub Actions badge link).
why_it_matters: A brand-new engineer with no prior context cannot determine where the source lives or how to acquire it from README alone. Confirmed by reading all 255 lines and extracting every local/relative doc link: only 'src/stealth_chrome_devtools_mcp/embedded/server.py' and 'LICENSE' are referenced; nothing about cloning or repo location.
fix_direction: Add a short 'Clone & Setup' subsection above 'Local Development' with the actual `git clone https://github.com/...` command and an explicit dependency-install step (e.g. `uv sync`) before the MCP-config snippet.

what: The only way to sanity-check this MCP-config snippet is to hand-translate it into an equivalent terminal command (README gives no other verification method). Doing exactly that on this checkout's real, unmodified path (which contains a bare `&` and spaces) and typing it unquoted -- as the bare placeholder `/path/to/stealth-chrome-devtools-mcp` gives no reason not to -- reproduced a real failure: PowerShell 7 parsed the trailing `&` as its background-job operator, silently launched a stray job against the truncated path '...CUSTOM MCPs', then threw a completely unrelated error: "The module 'PRODUCTIVITY' could not be loaded. For more information, run 'Import-Module PRODUCTIVITY'." Reproduced live; see audit/stage1/onboarding_trace.md step 1.3.
why_it_matters: This is a real, reproduced failure on the literal checkout path handed to a new engineer, with a maximally misleading error message (a bogus PowerShell module name) that sends them chasing entirely the wrong lead instead of a quoting problem. The doc gives zero warning that the directory value needs quoting when typed manually.
fix_direction: Add a one-line callout in the Local Development section: the --directory value must be double-quoted when typed manually in a shell (the JSON form itself is safe since MCP clients spawn argv arrays without a shell). Consider also recommending against '&'/space-bearing clone paths on Windows.

quote:
# Unit tests only (no Chrome needed)
uv run pytest -m "not integration"

# All tests (needs Chrome installed)
uv run pytest

# Verbose with short tracebacks
uv run pytest -v --tb=short
what: Running the exact first documented command, `uv run pytest -m "not integration"`, on this checkout fails immediately with `Failed to canonicalize script path` (exit code 1) -- no test collection, no stack trace, nothing else actionable. Reproduced identically three ways (piped output, unpiped, `uv run pytest --version`, and with an explicit quoted `--directory` flag). Bypassing uv entirely and invoking pytest directly through the checked-out venv (`.venv\Scripts\python.exe -m pytest -m "not integration" -q`) passes cleanly: 402 passed, 24 deselected, 1 warning in 61.91s. `uv run stealth-chrome-devtools-mcp` and `uv run stealth-chrome-devtools status`/`doctor` do NOT hit this error on the same path, so the break is specific to how `uv run` resolves the pytest console-script shim, not a blanket problem with this directory.
why_it_matters: This is THE documented test command -- the primary way any engineer, new or otherwise, is told to validate a change on this project. It is completely non-functional on this checkout as written, and the failure message has no discoverable connection to the real cause (a uv path-canonicalization limitation triggered by '&'/spaces in the folder name). There is no workaround, note, or warning anywhere in the docs. This blocks the single most important onboarding validation step on the exact checkout being audited.
fix_direction: Document the `.venv\Scripts\python.exe -m pytest` (or `python -m pytest` after activating the venv) fallback for checkouts uv can't handle. File/link the reproducible case against uv upstream. Short-term, add a README warning against '&'-bearing folder names for local checkouts on Windows.

quote:
## CLI

Installs a `stealth-chrome-devtools` ops command for managing the server and its
disk usage. (This is for *ops* -- to drive a browser, use the MCP server or its
HTTP backend.)

```bash
stealth-chrome-devtools status       # backend running? session root + caps
stealth-chrome-devtools profiles     # list profiles with size / role / in-use
stealth-chrome-devtools cleanup      # preview reclaimable disk (DRY RUN)
stealth-chrome-devtools cleanup --apply               # actually reclaim
stealth-chrome-devtools cleanup --session-cap-gb 12   # preview at a tighter cap
stealth-chrome-devtools doctor       # check Chrome / environment
stealth-chrome-devtools serve --http --port 19222     # start the server
```
what: Ran verbatim `stealth-chrome-devtools status` from the checkout root exactly as printed -> PowerShell: "The term 'stealth-chrome-devtools' is not recognized as a name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or executable program." The command only resolves once manually prefixed with `uv run` (`uv run stealth-chrome-devtools status` succeeds and prints backend/version/session-root info). The CLI block never distinguishes the pip-installed scenario (script on PATH, matches Quick Start) from the local-checkout scenario the 'Local Development' section two headings earlier set up.
why_it_matters: Directly under the local-development mental model the README itself establishes, its own CLI examples don't run as shown. A new contributor has to independently infer the `uv run` prefix, which is basic uv knowledge never stated in this document.
fix_direction: Prefix the CLI examples with `uv run`, or add a one-line note: "prefix each command with `uv run` when working from a local checkout instead of a pip install."

what: The `stealth-chrome-devtools-mcp` entry point named in this config is an MCP stdio server; README gives no other documented way to invoke or verify it manually. Ran it directly (path properly quoted this time) with `--help` appended, expecting either usage text or a clean exit. Instead: zero stdout/stderr, and the process did not return -- it was still running, having spawned multiple descendant processes, when the tool auto-backgrounded it after timeout and it had to be force-killed (`taskkill /T /F`, 8 processes).
why_it_matters: The single most natural verification step for a day-one engineer -- run the thing, see it respond -- is undocumented, and attempting it silently hangs with no diagnostic output, indistinguishable from a hang or crash without outside knowledge that stdio MCP servers block on stdin by design.
fix_direction: Point new contributors at `uv run stealth-chrome-devtools doctor` (F-404) as the actual safe, fast, human-facing way to verify a local install works, since the raw MCP entrypoint cannot be smoke-tested from a terminal by design.

quote:
## Testing

```bash
# Unit tests only (no Chrome needed)
uv run pytest -m "not integration"

# All tests (needs Chrome installed)
uv run pytest

# Verbose with short tracebacks
uv run pytest -v --tb=short
```

**256 tests** covering stealth arg filtering, profile resolution, orphan recovery, storage-cap sweeps, the ops CLI, and full browser integration.
what: No section, sentence, or link anywhere in README.md mentions linting, formatting, static type-checking, or a pre-commit/pre-push hook -- the entire quality-gate story is 'run pytest'. Confirmed no CONTRIBUTING.md, CHANGELOG.md, or docs/ folder exists anywhere in the repo either (recursive *.md search returned only README.md, CODEBASE_AUDIT.md, HIGH_VALUE_CHANGES.md, MCP_TOOL_TEST_RESULTS.md, and audit/ notes).
why_it_matters: For 'ship a one-line change', a docs-only contributor has no way to know whether the project expects ruff/black/mypy/anything before opening a PR. They either skip a check they didn't know existed (risking a CI surprise) or go spelunking in pyproject.toml on a hunch -- something the docs never point them toward.
fix_direction: Add a short 'Before you commit' subsection listing the exact lint/format/type-check commands actually enforced in CI, if any.

what: The README ends immediately after License with no Contributing or development-workflow section. There is no branch-naming convention, no PR checklist, no CI-gate description beyond a passing-tests badge image, and no release/versioning/publish process documented anywhere -- despite the package clearly being published to PyPI (Quick Start's `pip install stealth-chrome-devtools-mcp` plus the PyPI badge at the top of the file). No CONTRIBUTING.md or RELEASING.md exists in the repo (confirmed via the same recursive *.md search as F-406).
why_it_matters: For 'ship a one-line change', every step beyond running tests -- branch name, commit format, where/how to open a PR, who reviews it, how a merge becomes a PyPI release -- is pure guesswork drawn from outside convention rather than this project's actual practice, risking a rejected or reworked contribution.
fix_direction: Add a CONTRIBUTING.md (branch convention, commit style, PR expectations, what CI gates merging) and a short RELEASING.md (how a merge becomes a PyPI release -- version-bump location, tag format, whether the GitHub Actions workflow behind the Tests badge automates publishing).
